card.intuition: A directed graph has a cycle iff DFS finds a back-edge — an edge to a node currently in the recursion stack (GRAY). Use 3-colour marking: WHITE (unvisited), GRAY (in current DFS path), BLACK (fully processed).
card.analogy: Walking through a maze and marking your trail with chalk. If you encounter a chalked section before erasing it, you've walked in a loop. Once you've fully explored a dead end and backtracked, you erase that section's chalk.
when_to_use: Validating that a DAG has no cycles before topological sort
Detecting circular dependencies in build systems or imports
Deadlock detection in resource allocation graphs
gotchas: In directed graphs, a cross-edge (to a BLACK node) is NOT a cycle — only back-edges (GRAY neighbours) are
Undirected cycle detection uses a simpler visited+parent approach (see cycle-detection-undirected)
Kahn's topological sort is an alternative cycle detector: if output size < V, a cycle exists

exercise.prompt: Detect a cycle in a directed graph using 3-colour DFS.
hints: Recurse into WHITE (unvisited) neighbours: if dfs(v) returns True, propagate True upward immediately
